data.title: Big Bitcoin Bull Breakout In Progress (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
As I have been anticipating in recent articles, the next big bull run in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is now in progress. New all-time weekly and monthly closing highs are a stone's throw away, and once those levels have been cracked, the new bull will be in the open field. My targets for this move are $180k-$300k.

Some cite relatively high levels of "Greed" as measured by the Crypto Fear/Greed Index as a reason why BTC can't rally on from here.

While that can be the case under certain conditions, if we look back at the most analogous setup in the prior history of the Bitcoin market, we can see that the current situation of the peak of March 2021 and the breakout happening now is analogous to the period between the peak of December 2017 and the big breakout of September 2020.

If we overlay the Fear/Greed Index on the monthly chart, we find that the "Greed" level of today matches that which existed just as the 2020 rally was getting underway. It's worth noting that monthly RSI was also at the same level as today, back in 2020.

The structures and durations of both setups are remarkably similar, so it is not an out of distribution possibility that the results could be similar. Greed could reach maximum levels and stay there for quite some time as Bitcoin rallies hard. In the prior instance, that resulted in a 429% rally from the breakout point.

This situation is very similar in many respects to sentiment indicators such as the Bull/Bear ratio starting a bull run from a relatively high level, or technical indicators being already "overbought". Bears will often point out that a bull "can't" begin under such conditions...but this often turns out to be incorrect. Bullishness and "overbought" levels can persist for quite some time as a bull move continues unabated.

In this particular case, however, the technical condition of the Bitcoin market has plenty of upside wiggle room. The weekly chart, for example:

Here we can see that price is breaking out above the upper rail of the red flag consolidation pattern. Weekly RSI has lots of upside before getting into its resistance zone. The lengthy consolidation in the area of the prior all-time highs is extremely bullish.

Here's the daily:

Breaking out above the pattern. Daily RSI will reach resistance fast, but it can stay up there for a while, or even correct a bit, as the market continues to advance.

Short term:

Breaking out. Could get a little sticky in the 72-74k area as the last of the bears sell out. But after that, there's no more resistance, and the bull has a clear field to charge ahead.

No technical setup is completely unambiguous or a "slam dunk", but this one is among the stronger bull cases I have seen in almost 20 years of analyzing markets.

data.title: Ford: The Pessimism Has Gone Too Far
data.description:
Ford: Underperforming Peers And Market

Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) investors have had a highly challenging year, underperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) and its cross-town arch-rival, General Motors (GM). Therefore, the market seems to have delivered its verdict on CEO Jim Farley and his team (at least for 2024).

In my previous bullish Ford article in May 2024, I argued that Ford's increasingly profitable commercial business should help bolster its core business, even as the legacy automaker transitions to EVs.

However, Ford's EV growth roadmap has faced unanticipated stumbles, as the market likely assessed F as falling behind General Motors in its pursuit. Although Ford's core internal combustion engine business has continued to underpin its sector-leading profitability ("A+" profitability grade), the market seems unconvinced. Although F's attractive forward dividend yields should attract income investors to stay vested, growth investors have likely turned their attention elsewhere, given GM's more robust "C+" growth grade, well above F's disappointing "D-" growth grade.

Ford: Must Prove Its Path Toward Profitable EV Scaling

As someone who never likes to bet against the market, Ford investors must assess whether the market pessimism is reasonable, as automotive companies have undergone challenging growth deceleration over the past year. EV market leader Tesla (TSLA) has faced challenges reigniting its growth profile, likely worsened by its sky-high valuation metrics. Notwithstanding its expensive valuation, TSLA investors have outperformed Ford investors since April, as investors likely placed a higher emphasis on its growth recovery and autonomous driving and robotaxi ambitions.

Ford's ability to compete more effectively in EVs will likely be crucial in lifting the market's optimism through 2025. Ford will release its third-quarter earnings on October 28, affording investors an update on its business outlook. As a reminder, Ford is expected to ramp up its hybrid strategy, as the growth in pure-play BEVs has slowed markedly.

Ford reported a 0.7% increase in Q3 US sales, corroborating the market's caution. Q3's growth was significantly lower than the 2.7% average Ford notched YTD through September 2024. While the growth profile in hybrids (up 38% YoY in Q2) surpassed the growth rate (up 12% YoY in Q3) of BEVs, investors are likely increasingly concerned whether Ford's declining ICE business can help sustain its EV transition.

Ford: Growth Environment Has Turned More Competitive

As seen above, Ford has suffered a significant revenue growth deceleration since its peak in 2022. Furthermore, analysts expect Ford's sales to slow down through 2025, raising the execution risks in an increasingly challenging EV growth environment. Notwithstanding the EV transition risks, Ford's core ICE business is still profitable, critical to helping to sustain stable automotive adjusted EBIT margins through FY2026.

As seen above, Ford Blue (ICE segment) is still expected to post an adjusted EBIT of between $6B and $6.5B. However, it was revised downward from its prior outlook of between $7B and $7.5B. However, the strength of its commercial business (Ford Pro) is expected to mitigate the weakness of its EV business and the slowdown of its core ICE business. As a reminder, Ford Pro's adjusted EBIT outlook was revised upward in Q2, corroborating its growth momentum.

General Motors has also unveiled its mass-market EV model, demonstrating its resolve to compete more aggressively. GM's trajectory is notable, as the leading automakers have suffered several setbacks in China. Chinese EVs, led by BYD Company's (OTCPK:BYDDY) (OTCPK:BYDDF) incredible rise, have dented foreign automakers' market leadership as they struggle to transition effectively into EVs.

Notwithstanding my caution, Ford's downgraded Wall Street earnings estimates suggest a possible growth inflection point from FY2026. In addition, Ford's ICE business is still anticipated to be a core profitability driver, bolstering the confidence of income investors. Hence, it should help underpin F's relative undervaluation, suggesting much pessimism has likely been reflected.

While F's valuation grades have remained relatively undervalued compared to its consumer discretionary sector (XLY) peers, it has still underperformed. It's a critical reminder for investors not to focus solely on its valuation when assessing F's risk/reward profile.

As a reminder, F's adjusted EPS multiple of just below 6x is more than 60% below its sector median. However, it's markedly above its automotive peers' median of 3.6x. Therefore, Ford still needs to justify improved growth prospects relative to its sector peers. Despite that, its relatively low adjusted EPS multiple should also provide a valuation floor for the stock as the company attempts a more profitable transition for its EV business. In addition, its relatively low free cash flow multiple of just under 4x is more than 60% below its sector median. As a result, I assess that the market seems to have baked in significant pessimism, even as the company's ICE and Ford Pro business are still crucial profit drivers.

Ford's EV strategy has come under intense scrutiny as it faces an increasingly competitive EV market. The Chinese EV leaders are expected to up the ante in their quest for global domination. Therefore, Ford must justify its ability to demonstrate a clear path toward EV profitability. Thus, GM's ability to scale faster could impact the market's confidence in F, potentially affecting its ability to recover from its underperformance.

Despite that, the market sentiments toward automakers could improve through 2025, as the Fed is expected to turn increasingly dovish. With interest rate headwinds expected to have peaked in 2024, investors will likely bank on improved buying sentiments in the automotive business as the drag on large purchase consumer spending becomes less of a drag. Hence, it should also underpin F's relative undervaluation as the market assesses a potential valuation re-rating to levels more in line with its sector peers.

Is F Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

F's price chart indicates the stock has been mired in an extended consolidation zone above the $9 support level, while buying advances have struggled above the $15 level.

Therefore, I observed robust dip-buying support at F's October 2023 and August 2024 lows as investors loaded up. The stock's attractive forward yields should help underpin its appeal, as the Fed is expected to lower interest rates through 2025. Therefore, I assess its $9 support zone should remain constructive, allowing more aggressive opportunities to add exposure.

F's recent recovery from its August 2024 lows has remained resilient, notwithstanding its relative market underperformance. I assess that the market is likely looking for more proof points in Ford's EV growth recovery before lifting its battered valuation further. Despite that, its risk/reward still looks attractive, although investors must be cautious about expecting market performance through 2025.

Rating: Maintain Buy (under review).

Important note: Investors are reminded to do their due diligence and not rely on the information provided as financial advice. Consider this article as supplementing your required research. Please always apply independent thinking. Note that the rating is not intended to time a specific entry/exit at the point of writing, unless otherwise specified.

I Want To Hear From You

Have constructive commentary to improve our thesis? Spotted a critical gap in our view? Saw something important that we didn’t? Agree or disagree? Comment below with the aim of helping everyone in the community to learn better!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rate Cuts To Boost OneMain Financial
data.description:
OneMain Holdings (NYSE:OMF) is a stock I've covered twice before. The first article was my inaugural piece for Seeking Alpha, in which I went over the business model and why OMF was a leader and subprime lending. I closed that Buy thesis with:

While potential always exists for greater stress to hit their loan book than they have anticipated, OneMain Financial stands out as a deft reformer among subprime lenders in the years that followed the associated financial crisis. Today's buyers should be conservative and not expect quick returns. OMF will provide long-term value primarily through its dividend and modest capital appreciation.

In my second article, I maintain my Buy rating, noting OMF's sturdiness in a period of heightened rates and its ability to engage in M&A during these times:

While no materially major changes occurred in Q4, we did finally get the FY 2023 results, and (crucially) we finally saw the seeds of OneMain's future being planted: auto-finance and credit cards. Leveraging the expertise from their core business in personal loans, OneMain will be a position to grow its high-yield loan book as it enters these markets on favorable terms.

With challenges in the year so far and Q3 results on the horizon, I thought another update was in order. I think the year's progress speaks to OneMain's resilience, and I remain confident that it is a Buy as we approach greener pastures.

YTD 2024 Results

As I did not follow up after the first couple of quarters, let's take a look at the YTD results for 2024.

First thing to note is that net finance receivables continued to grow, in spite of the distress previously discussed by the company.

Much of this owes to the acquisition of Foursight Capital, which closed at the start of Q2. This acquisition allows OneMain to take their underwriting strengths and connect it to Foursight's auto-dealer networks.

Some of it can also be attributed to their other new product: the BrightWay credit cards. With the testing period done, OneMain is steadily rolling it out to new customers.

Delinquency rates have also been lower, after peaking in Q4 2023, suggesting the worst of the credit stress caused by the Fed's rate hikes and their cooling of the economy has passed.

Yet, this has not been only a matter of OneMain idly rolling with interest rates. It's a story of active portfolio management as well. As rate hikes began, the company began a credit tightening strategy, to write loans that had the risk parameters to endure a tougher macro-environment. These newer loans (the "front book") have contributed to the lower delinquencies as the older ones run off.

As the earnings are concerned, they remain positive but lower compared to years prior. As net interest income and insurance revenues are up, the main reason is the aforementioned delinquencies.

In spite of this, OneMain continues to feel comfortable about the health of its balance sheet, with buybacks and dividends continuing. They even increased their quarterly dividend by 4% in Q2.

Future Outlook

As I remain optimistic about the company, I'll lay out the near-term and long-term factors, as well as some of the risks that still exist as we enter a period of rate cuts.

Near-Term

Rate cuts will be the biggest influence in the near future. Speaking during the Q2 2024 earnings call, CEO Doug Shulman explained what generally occurs in a hypothetical where rates go down (reminder that this was before cuts were announced in September):

Consumer lending is a cyclical business. And you can see a world where the consumer is feeling a lot better. Interest rates are coming down; credits getting much better and a bunch of things could be moving in the right direction. We feel pretty good that even in a tricky environment, our metrics are now both moving in the right direction where we've seen a modest uptick in origination, and we see the credit trends moving in the right direction.

A month into our first cut and with another FOMC meeting coming in November, there's reason to think that OMF's portfolio will start to fare better.

Another reassuring sign is that real wage growth grew as inflation shrank and fell below the nominal wage growth. As this trend has persisted throughout 2024, I believe more borrowers one OneMain's book should be in a better position to pay in full. It also speaks well for newer originations going forward.

At the September Barclays investor conference, Shulman spoke to this as well, stating that the general consumer has "stabilized," even if they remain "skittish" at these nominally higher prices.

Long-Term

Reiterating somewhat from my original thesis, the real value of OneMain is in its steady value accumulation over time, not rapid growth. Shulman spoke to this once more in the earnings call:

I think, in terms of growth, we are being deliberate. We are not chasing growth across any of our products right now. We have very tight credit boxes across personal loans, auto and card. But as I've talked about before, Foursight gives us now a platform that as we scale this business deliberately, we'll be able to build it over time. I imagine the significant growth will happen once we feel that some of the macro uncertainty is cleared and things have turned fully.

This is evidenced by the steady stone-setting OneMain has undertaken during their period of credit tightening. They used the context of high inflation and rate hikes as a testing ground for their BrightWay product. They engaged in M&A while other lenders have been trying to salvage their balance sheets. These things don't produce growth in the year that they occur. They keep the business in the game so that they can consolidate their advantages.

Moreover, as the recently increased dividend yields in the high, single digits, a significant portion of the return will be these distributions over the lifetime of the business.

Valuation

My previous valuation used a method similar to Discounted Cash Flow and substituted free cash flow with conservative estimate of EPS.

Using earnings figures at the time as a guide (GAAP earnings and capital generation), I came up with $6.25 per share. From there I surmised that 3% EPS growth was an achievable over time as they steadily grow their balance sheet, repurchase shares, and expand with M&A. 3%, along with my terminal multiple of 5, were designed to make it harder to overvalue this business.

Above, I made only a slight change, reducing my baseline EPS to $6.00, in light of losses from delinquent loans this year. That reduces my calculation of intrinsic value from about $60.50 to $58. It's a minor difference but one I wanted to get down.

Additionally, I'll remind folks that both are priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of the market). As it stands, OMF remains below this $58 price.

Risks

Even with my optimism and efforts to avoid overvaluation, let's consider some more risks.

Referencing the skittishness consumers have faced, even as real wages have managed to catch up with inflation, this could indicate that the growth they intended after the cyclical swing in rates may not occur as quickly as they wish. Customers may spend more conservatively for longer than expected.

Another risk is regulatory. As these loans are issued at APRs well over 20%, many view OneMain's business as usury. Political pressure at the state or federal level could result in regulatory crackdowns that prevent OneMain from pricing their loans for the risk profiles of their customers.

Lastly, with the acquisition of Foursight, OneMain's results will now follow more the cyclical trends in the auto industry, and problems there could, to a greater degree than before, be a problem for OneMain.

Q3 Outlook

All these things considered, I don't expect many major changes to occur for Q3's results, which will be out on Oct. 30. I think the important details will be qualitative. How are their customers are feeling? How are they responding to the first rate cut? Will there be anything to worth reporting about the integration of Foursight into their auto line?

These are examples of the questions I ask myself as the earnings day approaches.

Conclusion

OneMain Financial continues to be the preeminent lender of subprime customers. 2024 has been a tough year so far, but the company has used this time well to prepare itself for the turn in the cycle, while not over-extending their balance sheet. Because of their religious risk aversion and attractive discount to its market price, I still consider it a Buy at these prices.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: Not Yet Low Enough
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

It's been some years since I last covered Leggett & Platt (NYSE:LEG). In my last article, which you'll have to go back around 3 years to find, I went to a "Hold" for this company and sold my position about 2 months after the fact as the fundamentals worsened. You can find the last article on the company here.

The result since that rating is the following, which means that I view it as the "right" sort of rating.

This is a non-trivial decline for this sort of company. We're essentially talking about a 110% underperformance that I avoided, and others avoided, by not investing in this company. This is no small feat, especially since at the time the company was actually a fairly high-yield dividend stock and one that was fairly popular by my estimate.

In this article, I'm going to provide a long-needed update on what has happened to Leggett & Platt and where we could see this company going from here.

My minimum rate of return, conservatively, that I am looking for is around 15% per year. This is something that we're looking at if I'm able to get here, and what the company has been doing for the past few years.

Let's see what Leggett & Platt has done here.

Leggett & Platt - The company's downturn is based on some fairly clear trends, and they're unlikely to significantly improve in the near term

Leggett & Platt is a 141-year-old company. For a company like this to take the sort of nosedive that we've been seeing, it's quite a few things that have to happen.

Leggett & Platt is a business that, when I wrote about it last and as I am writing about it now, focuses on the manufacturing, development, and sales of bedding products, furniture, flooring, textiles, and the like. It's working with 15 different business units, organized into three segments.

These engineered components and products are not "bad" per se. And looking at the company that manufactures them and its traditions, it's not necessarily bad either. But the last years since the pandemic has not been good for the company. It's not just competition either, from an international basis, but inflation and other trends as well.

The recent results we have for Leggett & Platt confirm the continued downturn and decline for the company. 2Q24 sales were at $1.1B, which is another increase of 8% YoY, with both a volume and a raw material price decline. EBIT was down as well, in this case over $20M since the last quarter, and the company's adjusted EBIT margin is down 1.2% - which is significant given that the company's EBIT margin is less than 7.5%, to begin with, and now down to 6.3%. EPS is down more than 10% as well, and the company has updated its sales guidance and EPS estimate.

Leggett & Platt is now expecting sales to go no higher than $4.5B, versus a prior expected level of $4.65, and an overall adjusted EPS of no higher than $1.25, but more likely around $1.1/share, according to my own forecasts.

Highlights and positives for the company were fairly slim. Every single KPI that matters was negative for the quarter. EBITDA was down almost 25%, and this is from an already fairly low level to begin with. One of the few positives was a very slight decrease in working capital QoQ and YoY, but 30 bps is so marginal that I wouldn't consider it to matter all that much here, to be honest.

Net debt, despite the company changing things up, is actually increasing due to lower EBITDA. We're now at 3.83x net debt/R12 adjusted EBITDA - again, yet another negative for the company.

So what really remains for Leggett & Platt here? We have a BBB- credit rating, we have over 64% long-term debt/capital despite significant dividend cuts, and we're down to a rate of market capitalization that doesn't even cover 30% of the annual sales, implying a high lack of confidence for this company as things stand. The yield, which was once at over 5%, is now down to 1.47% even with the cut dividend rate.

The risks I see to Leggett & Platt here are numerous - and they go beyond just the fact that most of this sort of manufacturing for Home Furnishing is now done in other geographies outside of the US, where LEG currently manufactures. It also has to do with cost inflation, asset age, with the company's sales trends, which are down. We can also point to automotive exposure, which is significant here, and overall exposure to the industrial macro, which again - exists. Furniture and bedding companies that focus on quality and local production can make things work - this has been proven by local manufacturers across the world, but in order for Leggett & Platt to make this work, they need to change up further.

So what are the company's plans for this? And above all, what is the company telling us that it's going to do?

Leggett & Platt points to things like its strong competitive local position with very few large competitors, and large addressable markets inside and out of the US. It's fairly historically focused, arguing for its history of cash generation, a solid balance sheet with long-term sub-2x Net debt targets, and again, its management history, knowledge, and understanding of the overall business.

LEG's restructuring plan is in full force, and operational efficiencies according to the company are on track. It's unfortunate, then, that these aren't more visible at this time. Though, to be fair, the company also says that it's "strategic review" is currently underway as well.

I've re-reviewed LEG now in terms of exposures, and what I expect. What worries me is the 45% exposure to Automotive and Industrial macro (these are not my numbers, these are the company's own numbers), with the remaining being consumer durables - although durables might be pushing it, given the competitive position that the company is in here.

The company's dividend cut and current other ambitions all have a number of goals. These include things like maintaining IG, (although they recently increased the RCF to allow for a 4x leverage ratio, meaning a $1.2B revolver is now there), and plans to repay some notes in November.

So how is this increase expected to be delivered during these next few years? Fairly simple - the restructuring plan, meeting the operational efficiency targets, and expected demand improvements - some of which are outside of the company's control. What is also beyond the company's influence is cost recovery of raw materials and pricing - given that pricing is also an external factor, and if other players change their pricing, this company might have to do so as well.

The aforementioned restructuring plan Is showing some promise - it would be unfair to say otherwise (shifting to some positives here). The company has moved all of its spring production to the locations that remain, completed its China innerspring downsizing, and closed a number of plants, with more consolidations coming. Also, several assets are currently on sale or sold, with another several planned. In furniture, the company has restructured as well, closing plants, moving the engineering team, and once again, selling/selling properties.

All In all, the company is moving forward with its plan, but what will remain after this plan is finished remains to be seen - all we have now are estimates, and here are the overall estimated impacts at this time.

The core question becomes, do we want to invest in a home furniture/bedding/industrial turnaround at this valuation and with this upside? Because, one positive I will be clear about here is the fact that we are actually at an extremely low valuation, compared to where the company was historically.

Leggett & Platt - Upside to a conservative growth rate, but with impaired fundamentals and issues

My last article on this company was 4 years ago. I view this company as an investment I managed to avoid, but one I was never close in pushing into at the time given what else was available.

The trend for the company since has been abysmal, a word I don't use lightly, but which is warranted here given what you can see here in terms of trends.

Going forward, it seems clear to me that at some point we might see a recovery - but even if we go by the rates of recovery forecasted here, we're not necessarily seeing a very impressive sort of overall trend for the next 2-3 years. At a conservative 12-13x P/E, the RoR is below 15% annualized - and that's inclusive of the meager yield the company offers here.

The simple fact is, I don't see the valuation-related upside here that I would be interested in seeing if I were to take the risk - and it is a risk - of investing in Leggett & Platt.

I do not believe the company is going bankrupt, and I do believe that there is value here in the common equity; ergo, there is a valuation where I would "Buy" this company. But in this sort of situation, I tend to look for 25-30% annualized RoR - and those are conservative, not optimistic. What I mean is that I would want 25-30% annualized based on a low implied P/E multiple, not something that's outside of the company's justified trading range.

I do not have that here - we do not have that here. What we have is 6-12% at 10-12x P/E, and around 15-17% annualized at 14-15x P/E - and that's far from enough for me.

I would be interested to wait and see what happens when 3Q and 4Q come out here - because I expect 3Q, which is coming in a relatively short while (hence the preview here) to confirm the continued downward trajectory in the face of not exactly improving macro. I believe it will take until early or mid-2025 until we see definite signs of a turnaround, maybe even longer - and that means I believe we have time until we need to decide whether to invest here.

Average analyst targets confirm my stance. I would give the company something of $15/share if things turn around, which means that we want a higher upside. But the 3 analysts following the company here go from $11 - $14/share, with an average of $12.5/share, and 3 out of them are at a "Hold" recommendation here. That means none of them believes it is time "yet".

I agree with this assessment, and give the company a rating in accordance with this, as seen below, for October 2024.

Thesis

  • Leggett & Platt could be a company you want to buy during a turnaround, provided that you do so at the right price. I do still like the company management, the way the company seems to handle its business, and the company's market-leading position as well as its very disciplined use of cash. Some of the issues the company has faced here is certainly outside of its sphere of influence, and this should be noted.
  • However, all that withstanding, the company is not exactly an outperformer, nor even an expected outperformer in the near term. While growth is expected to increase from here on out, it's not expected to increase in 2024 - and in fact, the company has recently negatively updated its guidance for the year.
  • Given the lack of visibility, the overall higher correlation to an unfavorable macro, including automotive, industrial, and furniture, a sub-par yield, BBB- credit, and its recent history, I rate Leggett & Platt as a "Hold" and add my voice to the choir of investors who are now on the sidelines.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

Quality is a question following the company's sub-par development. Its dividend is cut, but well-covered here - and it's not cheap. Its upside is speculative here, and I think it's better to wait. 2/5, and a "Hold".

data.title: BioNTech's Oncology Gamble: High Stakes, Big Potential
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered the German biopharma, BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX), in March. The company, known for its efforts in developing a COVID vaccine with Pfizer (PFE), is making progress in oncology. Recall that, in a recent slide deck, BioNTech highlighted a few oncology assets, like BNT323/DB-1303 (HER2), BNT316/ONC-392 (CTLA-4), BNT327/PM8002 (VEGF-A x PDL-1 VHH), BNT211 (CLDN6), and BNT311/GEN1046 (PD-L1 x 4-1BB). Based on BioNTech's valuation, I believed the market was notably pessimistic about their R&D efforts, particularly in oncology, but this was largely unfounded. I rated BioNTech as a "buy," citing their "boatload of cash" and "multiple shots on goal." Since then, BioNTech's stock has risen by 24%, nearly doubling the S&P 500.

BioNTech’s BNT327 Targets Resistant EGFR-Mutated NSCLC with Impressive Results

BNT327/PM8002 has recently garnered a lot of attention after Summit Therapeutics' (SMMT) ivonescimab (PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody) demonstrated PFS superiority over Merck's (MRK) Keytruda in a China-based Phase 3 trial in first-line NSCLC with PD-L1 expression ≥1%. This is the first time a bispecific antibiody has outperformed Keytruda in a head-to-head study, with findings indicating clinical benefit across multiple subgroups. However, FDA approval will hinge on further demonstration of survival benefits in broader, non-China-based trials. BioNTech's, in partnership with Biotheus, BNT327/PM8002 had demonstrated promising antitumor activity in Phase 2 trials. At ESMO 2024, BioNTech presented data on EGFR-mutated NSCLC progression following EGFR-TKI. In this difficult-to-treat population, the ORR for PD-L1-negative patients was 39.3% (n=28). As is common, responses increased in proportion to PD-L1 expression. This figure is especially significant in PD-L1-negative patients, as many therapies in this field have poor efficacy in this population.

The number of new EGFR-mutated NSCLC cases in the United States is estimated to be between 20,000 and 30,000 per year. Up to 25% of these patients will develop resistance to first-line EGFR-targeted therapies such as TKIs (e.g., Tagrisso), making a new therapy like BNT327/PM8002 particularly appealing.

BioNTech’s Lung Cancer Trial: A Partial Hold but Not a Full Stop

Last week, it was announced that the FDA slapped a partial clinical hold on one of their two Phase 3 candidates, BNT316/ONC-392, in the treatment of NSCLC.

The trial's independent data monitoring board noticed "varying results" between the squamous and non-squamous NSCLC patient population, raising concerns regarding the treatment's efficacy or safety. Partial holds pause the enrollment of new patients but allow existing patients to continue receiving treatment. So, whatever the issue is, it appears that it doesn't pose immediate harm but, rather, requires further clarification. This could be the result of the inherent biological differences between these populations. They can respond differently to drugs. Moreover, other trials involving BNT316/ONC-392, like in ovarian and prostate cancers, are unaffected. Investors should watch for some kind of resolution to the partial hold within the next few months before jumping to conclusions. These events are commonplace in drug development. In fact, the FDA just lifted a partial hold on one of BioNTech's Phase 1 ADC, BNT326/YL202. Meanwhile, the news caused minor weakness in BioNTech stock (closed down 2% or more), disrupting some of the momentum BNTX had gained since the beginning of September.

Financial Health

In Q2, BioNTech reported €128.7 million in revenues. The cost of sales was €59.8 million. R&D expenses were €584.6 million, compared to just €373.4 million in the same period last year. S&M and G&A expenses were €12.9 million and €170.9 million, respectively. The net loss for Q2 was €807.8 million or $878.2 million. These are significant figures and highlight the need for clinical breakthroughs.

Turning to their balance sheet, as of June 30, BioNTech had €10.376 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Current security investments were €6.916 billion. Total current assets were €18.281 billion, while total current liabilities were just €2.425 billion. This suggests a current ratio comfortably over 2, which indicates BioNTech can easily cover short-term obligations.

Although BioNTech generated €1.627 billion from operating activities in Q2, this figure cannot reasonably be utilized to estimate for cash runway. The company is, after all, unprofitable and makes major investments in R&D. For purposes of estimating cash runway, although unconventional, I will utilize the net loss figure for the first six months of 2024, which was €1.122 billion. This is equivalent to a cash burn of approximately €203 million every month. If we divide their cash and securities by this figure, it suggests a cash runway of over 7 years.

Recall that BioNTech made a killing during the pandemic, and this has allowed them to ramp up R&D efforts in oncology.

Note: Cash runway and burn estimates are based on historical data and my analysis and should be viewed as estimates only. The term "cash burn" can encompass various factors beyond cash flow, making these estimates somewhat subjective. If companies provide forward-looking estimates in their earnings releases, I will include them to supplement my analysis.

BNTX Stock: Risk/Reward Analysis and Investment Recommendation

BioNTech continues to make progress in its oncology pipeline, which is key for diversification beyond COVID-related revenues. Although crowded, their work in PD-1/VEGF may pay dividends, particularly in more niche areas like EGFR-mutated NSCLC that could still procure a major blockbuster. Meanwhile, setbacks like partial holds exemplify the risks inherent in drug development. Although BioNTech's technology holds water in their moat, the future is highly uncertain. COVID provided significant revenues, but this has since waned considerably. Their efforts in a COVID/flu combo shot, which could reasonably replace much of the $8 billion global flu vaccine market, may have suffered a setback as well, after the Phase 3 trial met only one of the two primary endpoints.

Still, I have to side with the mRNA technology that quickly developed a vaccine for a novel disease that ended up saving tens of millions of lives. BNTX is a Quadrant 1 investment (high risk/high reward) that merits a place in a barbell portfolio (maintain "buy"). However, their lucrative R&D spend will necessitate a clinical breakthrough within a couple of years to outperform the market. Judging by the recent updates, I'm willing to continue to give them the benefit of the doubt.

data.title: Tyra Biosciences: Upcoming FGFR3 Inhibitor Data Is A Major Inflection Point
data.description:
Tyra Biosciences (NASDAQ:TYRA) is gearing up to report data from its phase 1/2 study, using its FGFR3-inhibitor TYRA-300 for the treatment of patients with urothelial carcinoma and solid tumors. In particular, the company is set to release such data through an Abstract at the upcoming 36th EORTC-NCI-AACR Symposium on Molecular Targets and Cancer Therapeutics [ENA 2024]. It is expected that on October 25th of 2024, when the embargo ends, that the abstract data will be released. Should the interim data at the medical conference be good, then this will allow it to expand to a phase 2 study specifically targeting patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma and non-muscle invasive bladder cancer.

What really shines for this biotech is the fact that FGFR inhibition is not only capable of targeting patients with cancer. It may also be effective in skeletal dysplasia disorders. Having said that, it is gearing up to file an IND submission of TYRA-300 for the treatment of children with Achondroplasia [ACH] in the 2nd half of 2024. Even further than this, it is expanding this program to also use this drug to target patients with Hypochondroplasia [HCH]. With interim data of TYRA-300 targeting urothelial carcinoma and solid tumors to be presented this coming Friday, October 25th of 2024 at EnA 2024, plus the ability to move the very same drug towards two other non-oncology indications, I believe that investors could benefit with any potential gains made here.

TYRA-300 For The Treatment Of Patients With Urothelial Carcinoma

As I stated above, Tyra Biosciences is gearing up for a very important week. Especially, since it is expected that it will release interim results from its phase 1/2 SURF-301 study, using TYRA-300 for the treatment of patients with urothelial carcinoma and solid tumors. This study is looking to see if proof of mechanism of action [MOA] is accomplished in being able to help treat these patient populations. Having said that, the goal is to move this FGFR3 inhibitor forward into a phase 2 study, barring very positive data at the upcoming medical conference. The two patient populations to eventually be targeted in a phase 2 study are metastatic urothelial carcinoma and non-muscle invasive bladder cancer. Before going over the ongoing early-stage study, plus any catalysts to come out of this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what each type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunities could exist for them. Urothelial carcinoma [UC] is where malignant cells form on the lining of the urinary system. This particular type of cancer accounts for a majority of bladder cancer cases and 7% of all kidney cancer cases. The global urothelial carcinoma treatment market size is expected to cross $15.1 billion by the end of 2036. On the other hand, the other cancer targets a specific part of the urinary system. With respect to non-muscle invasive bladder cancer [NMIBC], malignant cells form on the bladder's inner lining. Even more importantly, the cancer has not been able to spread into the deeper muscle layers of the bladder. The global non-muscle invasive bladder cancer market is expected to reach $21.1 billion by the end of 2034.

In order to see if TYRA-300 can help treat either of these cancer patient populations, it has been running the phase 1/2 SURF-301 trial. The thing is that this is a very early-stage type of study. Why is that? That's because there is no placebo being incorporated into it. It is an open-label type of study attempting to explore the recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D], along with antitumor activity. As I noted in the beginning above, should the interim data to be released this Friday be good, then it will be in a position to move this program towards a phase 2 study with the RP2D of TYRA-300 targeting patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma and NMIBC. There is huge hope for this drug from the company, mainly because the MOA has been proven. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) has proven that its pan-FGFR inhibitor BALVERSA [Erdafitinb] can treat bladder cancer patients in adults, especially since it has been approved for it. Where does this leave Tyra Biosciences, though? The goal for it is that it may win on possibly not only the efficacy front, but in terms of safety. The reason why is because BALVERSA targets FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3 and FGFR4. On the other hand, TYRA-300 is only an FGFR3 inhibitor. The company believes that it can reduce off-target effects by selectively targeting this protein. Not only that, but it may also have a competitive advantage in being able to handle treatment emergent resistance mutations as well. If it can pull off some competitive advantages over BALVERSA with TYRA-300, then it could definitely do well in the market. There is another company with an FGFR inhibitor drug, and that would be Relay Therapeutics (RLAY). It is developing a drug with the very same MOA as these two companies, known as lirafugratinib [RLY-4008]. However, it is not intending to develop this drug on its own, and it is seeking a partner to finance further development of it.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Tyra Biosciences had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $373.8 million as of June 30th of 2024. The truth is that this company has plenty of cash runway, and I don't believe that it is going to raise cash in the next several months. Why is that? That's because it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into 2026. The company burns roughly $23.5 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $18 million in R&D expenses and then $5.5 million in G&A expenses. The only way a cash raise might happen is if the data to be released this Friday causes a huge surge in the stock price. Then, management might evaluate its options to raise some funds.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Tyra Biosciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the upcoming release of interim data from the phase 1/2 SURF-301 study, using TYRA-300 for the treatment of patients with urothelial carcinoma and advanced solid tumors. There is no assurance that the data to be released will turn out to be positive, for starters. Secondly, even if they are positive, there is no guarantee that the stock market will react accordingly, causing the stock price to trade higher.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the use of TYRA-300 for the other indications being explored, like Achondroplasia and hypochondriaplasia. An IND filing is expected to happen in the 2nd half of 2024 and there is no assurance that the FDA will allow a phase 1 study to begin for the treatment of patients with Achondroplasia. In addition, there is no way of knowing if the result to eventually be released from such a trial will be positive.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of competition for TYRA-300 for the treatment of patients with urothelial carcinoma. The goal for this biotech is to attempt to have fewer side effects and/or safety issues compared to BALVERSA. If this is not the case, then despite positive data being released, the notion that the drug doesn't have fewer side effects compared to an already approved drug could be disappointing. Plus, it is highly likely that TYRA-300 will also likely be compared to Relay's lirafugratinib drug as an FGFR inhibitor. Thus, the risk here is that the drug may not perform better than one or both of these other drugs in the same class.

Conclusion

Tyra Biosciences might be able to take what has been done with FGFR inhibitors thus far and enhance them. That is, to potentially remove the resistance that occurs, along with improving safety. This remains to be seen, but investors won't have to wait long to see whether or not TYRA-300 is successful in targeting patients with urothelial carcinoma. Positive interim data from the phase 1/2 SURF-301 study using this drug to treat these patients, would mean an ability to advance a phase 2 program targeting metastatic urothelial carcinoma and NMIBC patients. Furthermore, the drug made from this company is versatile, in that it could also be used to target skeletal dysplasias as well. Such other indications that can be explored with the use of TYRA-300 are Achondroplasia and Hypochondroplasia.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report October 20, 2024
data.description:
In this brief market report, we look at the various asset classes, sectors, equity categories, ETFs, and stocks that moved the market higher and the market segments that defied the trend by moving lower.

Identifying the pockets of strength and weakness allows us to see the direction of significant money flows and their origin.

Another week, another record high

The S&P 500 notched its 47th record high on Friday, reaching 5862. That puts the S&P 500 on the Top 10 list of years with the most new high days. Here's a look at the last 4 weeks.

A look at monthly returns.

This chart shows the monthly returns for the past year. October got off to a shaky start, but it is now solidly in the green.

S&P 500 returns by decade since the '50s.

For added context, here is a chart showing the average returns (CAGR) by decade since 1950. Our current decade is off to a good start.

During the decade beginning in 2000, an investor would have done better by avoiding the large cap S&P 500 stocks. Two big bear markets - the Tech bubble bursting and the Global Financial Crisis of 2008 - ouch.

At the opposite extreme, we find that the decade beginning in 1990 was the best an investor could have hoped for.

A look at drawdowns this year.

Here is a closer look at the pullbacks we've had in 2024, using a drawdown chart. The current drawdown is 0.0% after making a new high on Friday.

A look at the bull run since it began last October.

This chart highlights the 64.0% gain in the S&P 500 from the October 2022 low through Friday's close. We are firmly above the trendline and it looks like we may have further to run before this rally is over.

Major asset class performance.

Here is a look at the performance of the major asset classes, sorted by last week's returns. I also included the returns since the October 12, 2022 low for additional context.

The best performer last week was Blockchain, as Bitcoin rallied to a near record-high. The worst performer was volatility, as it continues to ebb from last month's mini spike.

Equity sector performance

For this report, I use the expanded sectors as published by Zacks. They use 16 sectors rather than the standard 11. This gives us added granularity as we survey the winners and losers.

Investors were buying Construction and Utilities, and selling Energy and Healthcare.

Equity group performance

For the groups, I separate the stocks in the S&P 1500 Composite Index by shared characteristics like growth, value, size, cyclical, defensive, and domestic vs. foreign.

The best performing group last week was Small Cap Value. Investors sold Foreign stocks and bought U.S. stocks.

The S&P Top 7

Here is a look at the seven mega-cap stocks that have been leading the market over the past year. These seven stocks account for 64% of the total YTD gain in the S&P 500. That's up from 53% just two weeks ago, reflecting the rekindled faith in the AI trade. Participation in the bull market has broadened on a year to date basis but is beginning to narrow again. NVDA (NVDA) was the hardest hit, while AAPL shined.

The S&P Top 7 dominance is reasserting itself

The 10 best performing ETFs from last week

Uranium miners are surging higher on the enticing promise of using nuclear power to run the mammoth server farms used by AI. Bitcoin miners also draw massive amounts of power from the grid.

The 10 worst performing ETFs from last week

The United States Oil ETF was the worst performer last week. China took a hit as hopes start to dim regarding the government's stimulus pledge.

The 10 best performing stocks from last week

Here are the 10 best performing stocks in the S&P 1500 last week.

Shares of Wolfspeed, Inc. (WOLF) have surged higher since it announced "State Street Corp's Strategic Acquisition of Wolfspeed Inc Shares."

The 10 worst performing stocks from last week

Here are the 10 worst performing stocks in the S&P 1500 last week.

Service Properties Trust (SVC) announced that it has "Reduced the Quarterly Distribution to $0.01 Per Share; $127 Million in Annual Savings Will Increase Liquidity.114 Sonesta Managed Focused Service Hotels Expected to be Sold in 2025; SVC Expects to Use Net Sales Proceeds to Repay Debt.Actions are Expected to Reduce Capital Expenditures and Leverage, Improve Portfolio Performance and Better Position SVC’s Hotel Portfolio for the Long Term." -Yahoo Finance

Final thoughts

To recap, in the week just past, investors were:

  • Selling large caps and buying small caps
  • Selling foreign stocks and buying U.S. stocks
  • Selling oil and buying uranium and gold
  • Selling energy and buying construction

My view is that long-term momentum is still healthy, but there will be pullbacks along the way - maybe even a 10% correction - until the presidential election is over and settled. Therefore, I'm advising clients not to chase stocks, but to wait for pullbacks to put fresh cash to work.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: I Am Loading Up On These +10% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

You are unique. There is no one else like you. There is no one else who possesses your particular set of talents, strengths, and gifts. Similarly, everyone's financial needs are unique. While one person might be content with $30,000 a year, another may struggle to find balance even with $1 million. The key to financial well-being isn't in comparing yourself to others, but in finding what works for you.

In that same vein, you shouldn't have to worry about things that aren't in your control. Factors like the economic outlook, market volatility, political landscape, and interest rate policy, among a myriad number of things, are not in the hands of an average investor. No matter how many hours you stay anxiously glued to the news cycle, things may not magically shift in your favor.

Many in the market have been actively trading based on their predictions of how quickly the Fed will cut rates. Even as the Fed itself did a poor job predicting how much it would raise rates and when it would start to cut, many investors revolved their trading around what the Fed would do and when.

Instead of trading based on what the Fed might do, we like to adopt a rate-agnostic portfolio by investing across the market, protecting our income, no matter what comes upon the economy. We want a portfolio that will produce a lot of income whether rates go up or down. Despite inflation continuing to ease, price pressures persist, raising doubts about the extent to which the Fed will continue to cut rates. Let us look at a pair of picks that let us benefit from either situation.

Pick #1: JPC - Yield 9.9%

Coming off one of the worst selloffs in the fixed-income market, many experts are calling this "a golden age of fixed income." Despite a considerable bounce from the bottom, this asset class offers a yield that would previously be available only when investors go way down in the credit spectrum or by going further out in the yield curve and taking on volatility from long interest-rate exposures.

Equities have done very well in recent years. But with interest rates dropping, Mr. Market does not recognize the weakness in the economy, and the S&P large cap sector trades at a forward PE in excess of 20x. Source

This is an extreme valuation in a slowing economy, requiring you to be defensive in your investments. We have often discussed exchange-traded preferred stock and baby bonds, which offer steady yields while improving overall portfolio defensiveness. Today, we will discuss a diversified CEF (Closed-End Fund) focused on a section of the fixed-income market mostly reserved for large institutions.

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) maintains investments in 320 preferred securities, mostly from issuers in the financial services sector. ~85% of JPC's portfolio is invested in diversified banks, capital markets, insurance firms, and regional banks. Source

What differentiates JPC from other fixed-income funds is the CEF's ~89% exposure to institutional preferreds that are sparingly available to individual investors since they trade thinly at a $1,000 par value. JPC's portfolio is strong in credit quality, with ~81% of its holdings maintaining investment-grade ratings.

The CEF maintains a well-balanced call exposure, offering protection to its earnings in a declining rate economy.

Additionally, JPC operates with ~38% leverage, which has considerably impacted its NAV and market prices due to the Fed's aggressive rate hikes.

The CEF's interest expense as a percentage of net assets shot up almost 8x since the bottom in mid-2021, reducing the funds available for distribution. Source

But with rates in their downward trajectory, we expect the same leverage to boost total returns from the continued recovery of the fixed-income asset class. Moreover, reducing interest expenses will result in higher funds available for distributions. In line with the strong recovery in anticipation of rate cuts, JPC was able to make a 40% distribution hike earlier this year. With a continued drop in borrowing costs, we expect this higher distribution level to be increasingly sustainable.

At current prices, JPC trades at a ~2% discount to NAV, making it a bargain for capturing a high 10% yield while increasing your portfolio defense.

Pick #2: SLRC - Yield 10.8%

ABF (Asset-Based Financing) continues to see growing popularity among alternative asset managers. In the broader private credit universe, Moody's estimates the addressable market to be ~$1.5 trillion and the trend to be in the very early stages.

According to Apollo Global, ABFs have historically experienced lower losses than corporate debt due to the nature of its underlying structures.

a) diversified underlying assets that mitigate single points of failure

b) strong documentation with covenants and cash-flow waterfalls

c) credit enhancement through bankruptcy remote and ring-fenced collateral

Since the Global Financial Crisis, banks have retreated from lending due to increased regulation, causing non-bank lenders to pick up this business. Alternative asset management leaders like Blackstone, Apollo, Sixth Street, and KKR are active in asset-based finance investments, actively expanding their footprint in this lucrative sector through M&A.

SLR Investment Corp. (SLRC) is a BDC (Business Development Company) with a comprehensive investment portfolio of ~$3.1 billion across 800 unique issuers. Measured at fair value, 99.2% of SLRC's portfolio consisted of senior secured loans, with 97.7% in first lien loans. The BDC's weighted average asset-level yield at the end of the second quarter was 11.7%

During Q2, SLRC originated $356 million and reported $292 million in repayments, resulting in net portfolio growth of $62 million. Notably, 87% of the originations were from Asset-Based Lending, life sciences lending, and equipment finance verticals, with ABL originations amounting to $130 million.

SLRC's management refers to ABLs as counter-cyclical investments that are well-positioned to benefit from potential economic headwinds. Non-accruals represented just 0.4% of the portfolio at fair value, and over 99% of the portfolio was rated 2 or better at the end of Q2 (with 2 being the risk rating at origination). Only one investment was in non-accrual status. Source

SLRC ended Q2 with $1.2 billion of debt outstanding with a net debt-to-equity ratio of 1.16x (within its target range of 0.9 to 1.25) and over $750 million of available capital to deploy. The BDC has $470 million of senior unsecured fixed-rate notes at a weighted average annual interest rate of 3.8%, and management has hinted at the possibility of accessing the investment-grade fixed-income market to raise additional capital to opportunistically handle either a durable or softening economy.

During Q2, SLRC reported an NII of $0.45/share, providing a 110% quarterly distribution coverage and the sixth consecutive quarter of NII exceeding distributions. This marks the highest NII in over five years, indicating diligent operations through a volatile post-pandemic economy. The SLRC team owns over 8% of the company's stock and includes having a significant percentage of their annual compensation invested in the BDC's common stock, indicating strong alignment in the interests of shareholders with that of management.

SLRC has room to leverage up to take advantage of market opportunities, and the BDC's investment-grade balance sheet positions it well to borrow favorably. SLR's strong allocation to first-lien loans and low non-accrual rates demonstrate the BDC's conservative approach and position it well to withstand persistent inflationary pressures, high interest rates, and other economic headwinds.

Conclusion

Successful investing is not about knowing everything that is ahead, but about anticipating the unknown. It is about adaptability and staying focused on your personal financial goals, rather than worrying about factors outside your control. This is why at High Dividend Opportunities, we maintain a deeply diversified portfolio of over 42 holdings, comprising ETFs, CEFs, BDCs, REITs, midstream MLPs, C-Corps, preferred securities, and baby bonds. Whether rates plunge or drop steadily, our portfolio is prepared to maintain the consistency of our income.

You shouldn't be planning for retirement hoping today's government schemes and plans stay intact to support your golden years. Nor should your strategy count on the markets to provide favorable prices all the time. Markets are inherently driven by emotions and will be volatile. By focusing on income, we sidestep the effects of market volatility in our retirement. This is the power of income investing.

data.title: The Dollar And Gold Firm
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY, USDOLLAR) is firm to start the new week. The Japanese yen and Australian dollar are the heaviest in the G10 (~0.30%). The euro and sterling are trading heavier but inside the pre-weekend range. The market anticipates the Bank of Canada to deliver a 50 bps rate cut in the middle of the week, and the Canadian dollar is threatening to extend its losses for the fourth consecutive week. China's prime lending rates were cut by 25 bps, slightly more than expected, and officials signalled there is scope for further easing before year-end. All but a few emerging market currencies are weaker today.

Outside of Japan and Hong Kong, the large equity markets in the Asia Pacific region were mostly firmer. Not so in Europe, where the Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is nursing a small loss after rising a little more than 1% over the past two sessions. US index futures are also softer. Bond markets are under pressure in Europe. Benchmark 10-year yields are up 4-5 bps. The US 10-year Treasury (US10Y) yield is nearly three basis points higher at 4.11%. Gold's run is extending into a fifth session today, the longest advance since a seven-day streak in March-April. December WTI is about 1.7% higher to near $70. It fell by nearly 8.25% last week, its largest fall drop in 4 1/2 years.

Asia Pacific

With a light regional economic diary, the focus is on whether Beijing will be announcing additional measures and the Japanese election on October 27. Chinese loan prime rates were cut by 25 bps today, the first cut since July, and a little more than expected. Still, given that other rates were cut in recent weeks, today's move seems like catching up rather than breaking new ground. The signal from officials is that further rate cuts will be considered before year-end and could include another cut in reserve requirements. Meanwhile, surveys by the local media warn that it is possible that Japan's LDP falls shy of the number of seats (233) to secure an outright majority in the lower chamber of the Diet. The last time that happened was in 2009. Still, the LDP is most likely to continue to govern with its coalition partner Komeito. Shortly after the election, the government is expected to announce a package of measures that cushion the impact of inflation and support growth. The extra budget will likely be bigger than last year's (JPY13 trillion, ~$87 bln).

The dollar made a marginal new high against the yen (~JPY150.35) since early August, last Thursday. The 10-year US Treasury yield rose to almost 4.12% ahead of the weekend, its highest level since early August, before turning down and settling lower. The greenback set session lows slightly below JPY149.40 before the weekend and fell to about JPY149.10 in the local session today before recovering slightly through to JPY150 in the European morning. As the dollar traded around JPY150 last week, Japanese officials appeared to step up their rhetoric about watching the market closely and reminding the market that "it is important for exchange rates to reflect fundamentals and move stably." The intraday momentum indicators are stretched, suggesting a limited upside in the North American morning. Encouraged by rising Chinese equities and the firm local employment report last week, the Australian dollar traded firmly over the past two sessions. It initially saw a marginal extension to almost $0.6725 before reversing lower. It has been sold through the pre-weekend low (~$0.6695), for an outside day. The close is important from a technical perspective, and a weak close warns that last week's low near $0.6660 is vulnerable. The US dollar's pullback ahead of the weekend, and especially against the yen, saw it unwind its gains against the offshore yuan. It has tested the upper end of the month-and-a-half range near CNH7.1250 before being sold to slightly below CNH7.11. The dollar slipped slightly below there today before recovering to almost CNH7.13. Last week's high was closer to CNH7.15. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.0982 (CNY7.1274 Friday; last week's fixing range was CNY7.0723 to CNY7.1274).

Europe

Europe's economic calendar is light this week, and the main feature is the preliminary PMI. There may be scope for a small uptick in the eurozone's results. Nevertheless, the swaps market has upgraded the chances of a 50 bps cut at the next ECB meeting on December 12. It is now near 30%. While the initial estimate of Q3 GDP (October 30) will draw attention, more import is the preliminary estimate of October CPI due the following day. Outside of the PMI, the UK has a light diary in terms of market-moving reports. The next important event is the October 30 budget that has caused so much consternation. Meanwhile, the swaps market remains highly convinced that the Bank of England will cut the base rate a quarter-point at the November 7 meeting and about a 73% chance of another cut in December, up from around 45% a week ago and before the softer than expected CPI.

The euro snapped a four-day drop before the weekend. It posted an inside day and remained below both the five- and 200-day moving averages, which have converged slightly above $1.0870. The area has capped it so far today, too. The euro has not settled above the five-day moving average (~$1.0860 today) so far this month. Options for 1.15 bln euro expire today at $1.0885. Sterling rose in back-to-back sessions to close last week. It was the first consecutive advance since a five-day rally ended September 24, but it is struggling today. It is softer but within the pre-weekend range. It was sold from $1.3060 to Asia Pacific to almost $1.3010 in the early European turnover. A two-month low was set last week near $1.2975.

America

The US reports the September index of Leading Economic Indicators today. So what? It is one of many indicators that has not been able to make sense of the post-Covid economy. The last month that the LEI rose was February 2022. The Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker was revised to 3.4% (from 3.2%) last week. The first official estimate is on October 30. Reports suggest that the US election is becoming a more important market factor. The closeness of the election polls at this late stage is seen as making Trump's reelection more likely, and the impact of his tariff proposals could be significant. Defecting further from the international trade regime that the US was instrumental in erecting is seen hurting the US closest trading partners the most (Canada and Mexico). The Bank of Canada meets on Wednesday. The soft CPI (below 2) and the signal from Governor Macklem that, after three rate cuts, the pace could be accelerated, has emboldened the market to discount a little more than a 90% chance of a half-point cut.

The US dollar settled above CAD1.3800 before the weekend for the first time in two-and-a-half months. It remains bid today. With a 50 bp cut expected in the middle of the week, and the risk that officials leave the door open to another large cut, it would seem to deny incentives to buy Canadian dollars. Last week's high was near CAD1.3840, and overcoming it could target the CAD1.3900 area. The year's high was set in early August around CAD1.3945. The greenback was sold to a three-day low against the Mexican peso (~MXN19.6515) before reversing higher at the end of last week. It settled near session highs around MXN19.91 and has reached almost MXN19.98 today. Mexico is seen as a country that is particularly vulnerable to a second Trump term. Last week's high was near MXN20.0280. Last month's high was slightly below MXN20.15. And the year's high set in early August was around MXN20.2180. Meanwhile, Brazil President Lula has canceled plans to attend the BRICS summit due to a head injury, but recall that at the end of last week, Brazil imposed new duties on a wide range of products from China and other Asia counties (e.g., iron, steel, fiber optics). Most of the tariffs are for six months, while the Ministry of Foreign Trade investigates further.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weekly Japanese Government Bond And Yen Simulation, October 18, 2024: Near Zero Term Premium Out To 10 Years
data.description:
Summary

Author's Note

This simulation has been done jointly with a U.S. Treasury yield simulation in a way that reflects the correlation among the 12 factors driving yields in each country. For more on the companion U.S. Treasury simulation, please contact the author. In addition, foreign exchange rates include very substantial idiosyncratic risk that is independent of interest rate factors. Both this idiosyncratic risk and the JGB and the U.S. Treasury yield simulations impact foreign exchange rates, resulting in the following distribution of the yen/U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward:

Pricing for short- and long-dated European options to buy yen versus U.S. dollars at a strike price of 149.48 for quarterly maturities out to 20 years is shown in the following graph. Note that the pricing for American options is the lower bound on fair-value pricing.

This Week's Simulation of Japanese Government Bond Yields

As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow's book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market's expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in the graph below, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current JGB prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is near zero for maturities of 10 years and under. It then increases steadily for maturities over 10 years, taking on a magnitude similar to most other bond markets in major countries.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through September 30, 2024 given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and JGB Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this week's Japan forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of JGB yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing JGB yield curve published daily by the Japan Ministry of Finance and other information sources. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday's implied forward rate curve shows 1-month rates at an initial peak of 0.65%. As maturities lengthen, there is a steady increase in forward rates, hitting 3.39% at the end of the 30-year horizon.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 100,000 future paths for the JGB yield curve out to twenty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted JGB Yields: 15.8% Probability by October 6, 2034

A large number of economists have concluded that a downward sloping yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers.

We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon JGB yield is lower than the 2- year par coupon JGB for every scenario in each of the first 80 quarterly periods in the simulation.[1] The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield is near zero in the near term, but it peaks at 15.8%, compared to 16.1% last week, in the 91-day quarterly period ending October 6, 2034.

Negative 3-Month Yields: 25.3% Probability by April 16, 2027

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next 3 decades. The probability of negative rates peaks in the near-term at 25.3%, versus 26.9% last week, in the period ending April 16, 2027 and stays elevated at or above 12% thereafter.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank in the United States on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investor, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term JGBs with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year JGB purchased at time zero at par value of 100 yen. We analyze default risk for four different initial market values of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85 or 80) represents a "strike price" on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory take-over (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The chart below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset's maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 45.85%, versus 46.02% in the prior week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the JGB yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

JGB Yield Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week's simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month bill yield in the JGB market in ten years is from 0% to 1%, unchanged from last week. There is a 30.74% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, compared to 30.61% one week before. Note that the probability of rates in the next-higher one percent range is also high. For the 10-year JGB yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, also unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 26.13%.

In a recent post on Seeking Alpha, we pointed out that a forecast of "heads" or "tails" in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be "heads" is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section, we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month bill rate and the 10-year JGB yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps[2]. We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in 1 percent "rate buckets." The forecast for 3-month bill yields is shown in this graph:

SASdistributionJGB3m20241018.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 21.51%. The probability that the 3-month yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 22.53% plus 2.55% plus 0.11% plus 0.00% = 25.20% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme works like this:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The Japanese government bond yield curve was simulated jointly with the U.S. Treasury yields and other government yields based on daily data since 1974. As a result, movements in JGB yields have a correlation with other government yields that is consistent with historical yield curve movements. Appendix: JGB Yield Simulation Methodology

The probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it briefly first.

Step 1: We take the closing JGB yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. We note in the following graph that JGB yields span (by rate level and maturity) only 45.75% of the historical experience in 14 countries:

For the highest degree of realism in a forward-looking simulation, using the international database is essential. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through September 30, 2024, we conclude that 12 "factors" drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January 2022.

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 100,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We "validate" the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting JGB curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 100,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% "buckets" displayed in the graph.

Do Nominal Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on SeekingAlpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long-term bonds than by rolling over short-term Treasury bills in the United States. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast. This study is being updated using the 14-country data set in coming weeks.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The JGB historical data is provided by the Japan Ministry of Finance. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 107,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a JGB data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow and Andrew Morton in 1992:

The no-arbitrage foreign exchange rate simulation is based on this well-known paper by Amin and Jarrow:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow's book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the "HJM" model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year yield cannot be derived from the initial 30-year term structure of yields.

[2] The actual simulation uses 91-day time steps and spans a 20-year time horizon.
